pattern.name: empty-state
pattern.summary: Communicates what will appear in a space once data is available and guides the user toward a productive next step.
pattern.description: Empty states are moments in an app where there is no data to display to the user. They are most commonly seen the first time a user interacts with a product or page, but can be used when data has been deleted or is unavailable. With just enough contextual guidance, empty states ensure a smoothness of experience, especially when things aren't working as expected.

guidelines.description: The empty state is composed of an optional illustration, a title, body copy explaining the next action, and optional primary and secondary calls to action. Elements are left-aligned as a block, with the exception of small tiles where the image is centered above left-aligned text.

parts.name: image
parts.description: A non-interactive illustration that relates to the situation. The image choice should reflect the context — use just text if space is limited. Decorative images MUST be given an empty `alt` attribute so screen readers skip them.

parts.name: title
parts.description: A short and concise explanation of the empty state. Where possible, write this as a positive statement (e.g., 'Start by adding data assets' rather than 'You don't have any data assets'). Alternatively, acknowledge the absence while implying a future state (e.g., 'You don't have any data assets yet').

parts.name: body
parts.description: Explains clearly the next action to populate the space. May also explain why the space is empty and include the benefit of taking the recommended step. Three options for directing the user: a primary action button, a link in the copy, or an instruction pointing to a UI element.

parts.name: primary-action
parts.description: A button or link referenced in the body copy that performs the most important next step (e.g., 'Create report', 'Add data source', 'Upload file'). When multiple empty states appear simultaneously, use a tertiary button to avoid multiple primary buttons on the page.

parts.name: secondary-action
parts.description: An optional secondary call to action, typically a link to documentation or a related resource (e.g., 'Learn more about reports'). Positioned below the body text.

presentation.alt: An annotated diagram of the empty state pattern with numbered callouts: 1. Image, 2. Title, 3. Body, 4. Primary action button, 5. Secondary action link.

items.name: type
items.description: The category of empty state, determined by the reason the space is empty. Each type has different content guidelines and levels of detail.

values.name: no-data
values.description: The most common first-use empty state. Explains what will be available in the space when data has been added, and provides guidance for the next step the user can take to populate it.

useCases.description: When the user has not yet created or added any data to this area of the product.
When the user has cleared or deleted all data from a previously populated space.

values.name: user-action
values.description: Occurs as a result of a user action — for example, a search with no results or a success confirmation after completing a process. Explains the reason for the empty state and provides follow-up steps.

useCases.description: When a search or filter returns no matching results.
When a user has successfully completed a process and there is nothing further to act on (e.g., all alerts resolved, inbox empty).

values.name: error-management
values.description: Occurs when data exists but cannot be surfaced — permissions issues, system failures, or missing configuration. A higher level of specificity is needed so the user can address the issue themselves. Express error messages in plain language, precisely indicate the problem, and constructively suggest a solution.

useCases.description: When the user does not have permission to view the data.
When a connected system or service is preventing data from loading.
When further configuration is required before data can appear.

items.name: size
items.description: The visual treatment adapts to the available space. Larger spaces allow illustration and more detail. Smaller spaces use text-only treatments to conserve space.

values.name: small
values.description: For small tiles and compact containers. Image is centered above left-aligned text. Use text-only if the image would dominate the space.

values.name: medium
values.description: For side panels, medium tiles, and data table bodies. Image may appear to the left of the text block or above it. Left-aligned as a block.

values.name: large
values.description: For full-page empty states and large content areas. Image appears above the text block. Two positioning options: use a wider left margin or center the block in the empty space.

items.name: empty
items.description: The default state when no data is available. The empty state illustration, title, body, and actions are displayed. The content that would normally occupy this space (table rows, card grid, list items) is not rendered.

items.name: populated
items.description: Data is available and the empty state is not shown. The normal content (table, grid, list) is rendered in its place. The transition from empty to populated should not animate — the empty state is simply replaced.

items.name: loading
items.description: Data is being fetched. Show a skeleton or loading indicator in place of the empty state. Do not show the empty state while loading — the user should not see 'no data' before the system has finished checking.

items.name: error
items.description: A system error occurred while fetching data. The error-management variant of the empty state is displayed with a specific error message and corrective action. If the error is transient, include a retry action.

items.trigger: Page or container loads with no data available.
items.description: The empty state is rendered in place of the content area. If there is a primary action, it is the first focusable element within the empty state region.

items.trigger: User activates the primary action (e.g., 'Create report').
items.description: The system initiates the creation flow — this may open a dialog, navigate to a new page, or begin an inline creation experience. The empty state remains visible until the action completes and data becomes available.

items.trigger: Data becomes available (creation completes, import finishes, or data syncs).
items.description: The empty state is replaced by the populated content. Focus is not moved — the user continues from their current position. If the user was in a dialog that is now closing, focus returns to the element that triggered the dialog.

items.trigger: User searches or filters and no results match.
items.description: The user-action variant of the empty state replaces the previously populated content. The message suggests adjusting the search terms or filters. The search field retains focus so the user can immediately adjust their query.

items.trigger: A system error prevents data from loading.
items.description: The error-management variant of the empty state is displayed. The message identifies the problem in plain language and provides a corrective action (e.g., 'Check your permissions', 'Retry', 'View activity log'). If a retry button is present, it is the first focusable element.

useCases.description: When a page, tile, data table, side panel, or other content area has no data to display — whether because the user hasn't added any yet, a search returned no results, or a system issue is preventing data from appearing.
When a user has successfully completed all tasks in a queue or resolved all items in a list, and the space is now intentionally empty (e.g., 'All caught up — no new notifications').
When the feedback is a successful outcome of a specific action (e.g., 'Changes saved', 'Account created').

alternative.name: success-messaging
alternative.rationale: Success feedback uses success colors and icons. Reusing the empty state pattern for positive outcomes confuses the visual language.

useCases.description: When the entire page fails to load due to a critical system error (e.g., 404, 503).

alternative.name: error-page
alternative.rationale: Full-page failures should replace the entire page shell with a dedicated error page that provides navigation back to a working area, rather than rendering an empty state inside a broken layout.

useCases.description: When data is loading and will arrive shortly.

alternative.name: loading-skeleton
alternative.rationale: Show a skeleton or loading indicator while data is being fetched. Never show 'no data' before the system has finished checking.

items.guidance: The empty state MUST replace the element that would ordinarily show. For example, an empty state for a table replaces the table — column headers and footer should not be present.
items.rationale: This avoids having a screen reader read the entire table structure before reaching the message that there is no content. It also prevents the visual awkwardness of an empty table with headers but no rows.

items.guidance: Be specific about what will be available in the space when data is there.
items.rationale: Users need to understand what they're missing and what to expect. 'No reports yet' is better than 'Nothing here' because it tells the user what kind of content this area holds.

items.guidance: If there is an actionable next step, include a direct link in the message copy or a primary action button to make that action fast.
items.rationale: As a general rule, don't lead the user into a dead end. If there are useful next steps, include them. An empty state without an action forces the user to figure out what to do next on their own.

items.guidance: Do not cover multiple options in one empty state. If there are multiple things a user can do, pick the most important and keep the focus on that.
items.rationale: Too many choices create decision paralysis. A single clear call to action gets the user moving faster than a menu of options.

items.guidance: When multiple empty states may appear simultaneously (e.g., multiple dashboard widgets failing to load), use tertiary buttons for calls to action instead of primary buttons.
items.rationale: Multiple primary buttons on a single page compete for attention and violate the one-primary-action-per-surface principle.

items.guidance: Error messages MUST be expressed in plain language, precisely indicate the problem, and constructively suggest a solution. Do not use error codes as the primary message.
items.rationale: Users need to understand what went wrong and what they can do about it. 'Error 403' means nothing to most users. 'You don't have permission to view this data — contact your admin to request access' is actionable.

items.guidance: Do not show the empty state while data is loading. Show a skeleton or loading indicator until the system has confirmed there is no data to display.
items.rationale: Showing 'no data' before the fetch completes creates a flash of incorrect content that erodes trust. Users may think their data is missing when it simply hasn't arrived yet.

items.guidance: Empty state illustrations MUST be given an empty `alt` attribute or `role="presentation"` so screen readers skip them.
items.rationale: Empty state illustrations are decorative — they do not convey information that isn't already present in the title and body text. Announcing them wastes screen reader users' time.

keyboardInteraction.action: Moves focus to the primary action button (if present), then the secondary action link (if present).
Activates the focused action — triggers the primary button or follows the secondary link.

ariaAttributes.description: The empty state container SHOULD use role='region' with an aria-label to identify the empty content area, so screen reader users understand they are in a distinct section of the page.
When role='region' is used, aria-label should describe the empty area (e.g., 'Reports — no data available'). This provides context for screen reader users navigating by landmarks.

guidelines.screenReaderBehavior: When the empty state appears, screen readers should announce the title and body text in sequence. The illustration is skipped (empty alt or role='presentation'). If a primary action is present, it is reachable via Tab. When the empty state is replaced by populated content, no announcement is made — the user discovers the content through normal navigation.
guidelines.focusManagement: When the empty state replaces previously populated content (e.g., after a search returns no results), focus is not moved — it remains on the element that triggered the change (e.g., the search field). When the empty state is the initial state of the page, focus follows the normal document order. When the empty state includes a primary action, that button is the first focusable element within the empty state region.

labels.definition: Use when the empty state encourages the user to make a new object from scratch.
labels.usage: Combine Create with the object being created (e.g., 'Create report', 'Create workspace'). Prefer over 'Add' when the object doesn't exist yet.
labels.context: Primary action button in no-data empty states

labels.definition: Use when the empty state introduces a multi-step onboarding or setup flow.
labels.usage: Use as a standalone label when the next step is a guided flow rather than a single action. Avoid when there is a more specific verb available.
labels.context: Primary action button in first-use empty states

labels.definition: Use when a transient error may be resolved by attempting the operation again.
labels.usage: Only use when the action is genuinely retryable (e.g., network timeout). Do not use for permanent errors like permissions issues.
labels.context: Primary action button in error-management empty states

labels.definition: Opens additional contextual information, typically external documentation.
labels.usage: Use as secondary action text only. Always pair with meaningful context when space permits (e.g., 'Learn more about reports').
labels.context: Secondary action link in any empty state variant

localization.description: Empty state titles and body text may expand 20-30% when translated. Ensure the layout accommodates longer text without truncation — the body text area should grow vertically rather than clip.

For RTL languages, the entire empty state block is mirrored. Text is right-aligned, and any directional illustrations (arrows, progress indicators) must be flipped. Non-directional illustrations (abstract shapes, generic icons) should not be flipped.

links.name: button
links.role: Primary and secondary call-to-action triggers within the empty state.

links.name: link
links.role: Secondary action links to documentation or related resources.

links.name: alert
links.role: Used in error-management variants to display structured error information when the empty state requires more detail than the standard body text provides.
